questions.question: What is the generally accepted upper limit for normal ovarian volume in a premenopausal woman?
questions.detail: Ovarian volume is considered normal up until 15 ml, though some sources allow up to 20 ml.

questions.question: A postmenopausal ovary is considered abnormally large if its volume exceeds:
questions.detail: The postmenopausal ovary should typically not be larger than 6 cc.

questions.question: In the early follicular phase of the menstrual cycle, follicles are typically smaller than:
questions.detail: Follicles seen during the early menstrual cycle are typically small (< 5 mm in diameter).

questions.question: By mid-cycle (around day 10), a 'dominant follicle' typically reaches a size of approximately:
questions.detail: By mid-cycle, the dominant follicle grows significantly, reaching about 20 mm.

questions.question: According to high-yield radiology terminology, an ovarian structure is classified as a 'cyst' only if it measures greater than:
questions.detail: Structures < 1 cm are follicles; 1–2 cm are dominant follicles; and > 3 cm are termed cysts.

questions.question: The 'Cumulus Oophorus' is a collection of cells in a dominant follicle that indicates:
questions.detail: The presence of the cumulus oophorus signals that ovulation is imminent.

questions.question: Theca Lutein Cysts typically appear on ultrasound as having a:
questions.detail: Theca lutein cysts characteristically show a multilocular cystic 'spoke-wheel' appearance.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT typically associated with the development of theca lutein cysts?
questions.detail: Theca lutein cysts are related to b-HCG overstimulation, which is seen in molar pregnancies, multifetal pregnancies, and fertility therapy, but not Turner syndrome.

questions.question: Ovarian Hyperstimulation Syndrome involves theca lutein cysts and which classic extra-ovarian finding?
questions.detail: OHSS findings include large theca lutein cysts as well as ascites and pleural effusions.

questions.question: A 'Paraovarian Cyst' is distinguished from an ovarian cyst because it:
choices: Does NOT distort the adjacent ovary
questions.detail: Paraovarian cysts are located adjacent to the ovary but do not distort the ovarian parenchyma itself.

questions.question: Paraovarian cysts are considered remnants of which embryological structure?
questions.detail: The paraovarian cyst is a congenital remnant arising from the Wolffian duct.

questions.question: When is the best time to perform a PET scan on a premenopausal woman to minimize physiologic ovarian activity?
choices: The first week of the menstrual cycle
questions.detail: Premenopausal ovaries can be PET-hot during ovulation; imaging during the first week of the cycle minimizes this activity.

questions.question: Unlike premenopausal ovaries, postmenopausal ovaries on a PET scan should:
choices: Be twice the size of the liver
questions.detail: Postmenopausal ovaries should not demonstrate significant uptake on a PET scan.

questions.question: An incidental simple-appearing ovarian cyst on a premenopausal ultrasound requires no follow-up if it is smaller than:
questions.detail: For premenopausal women, a simple cyst measuring < 7 cm requires no follow-up.

questions.question: A simple incidental ovarian cyst on a postmenopausal ultrasound requires follow-up in 3 months if it is larger than:
questions.detail: In postmenopausal women, simple cysts > 5 cm warrant a 3-month follow-up ultrasound.

questions.question: Which of the following is part of the 'Sinister Six' of common benign ovarian masses?
questions.detail: The 'Sinister Six' of common benign masses includes follicles, corpora lutea, hemorrhagic cysts, endometriomas, dermoids, and PCOS.

questions.question: A 'Corpus Luteum' can often mimic an ectopic pregnancy on ultrasound because it demonstrates:
choices: A 'ring of fire' (intense peripheral blood flow)
questions.detail: The corpus luteum classically demonstrates intense peripheral flow known as a 'ring of fire'.

questions.question: How can a corpus luteum be distinguished from an ectopic pregnancy during a 'push' maneuver with the transducer?
choices: Both will stay attached to the ovary.
The corpus luteum moves WITH the ovary, while an ectopic moves SEPARATELY.
The corpus luteum will turn into a fetus.
Both will move to the other side.
questions.detail: An important differentiator is that the corpus luteum moves with the ovary, whereas an ectopic pregnancy moves independently.

questions.question: Which Doppler finding is considered specific (though not sensitive) for ectopic pregnancy?
questions.detail: An RI measuring less than 0.4 or greater than 0.7 is a specific indicator for ectopic pregnancy.

questions.question: The 'Shading Sign' on T2-weighted MRI (T2 shortening) is a classic buzzword for which pathology?
questions.detail: The shading sign (T2 shortening in a T1-bright lesion) is pathognomonic for an endometrioma.

questions.question: What is the traditional clinical triad associated with endometriosis?
questions.detail: The triad consists of infertility, painful periods (dysmenorrhea), and painful intercourse (dyspareunia).

questions.question: If an endometrioma is going to undergo malignant transformation, the most sensitive MRI finding is:
questions.detail: An enhancing mural nodule is the most sensitive imaging feature suggesting malignancy within an endometrioma.

questions.question: Malignant transformation of an endometrioma is rare (<1%) but typically results in which types of cancer?
questions.detail: Malignant transformation typically yields endometrioid or clear cell carcinoma.

questions.question: 'Decidualized Endometrioma' refers to a solid nodule with blood flow found in an endometrioma during:
questions.detail: Decidualization is a malignancy mimic that can be seen in endometriomas during pregnancy.

questions.question: Polycystic Ovarian Syndrome (PCOS) is characteristically associated with a 'string-of-pearls' appearance and ovaries that are:
questions.detail: In PCOS, the ovaries are typically enlarged with a volume > 10 cc.

questions.question: The classic ultrasound descriptor for hair within an ovarian dermoid (teratoma) is the:
questions.detail: The 'dot-dash' pattern is a characteristic ultrasound finding for hair fibers within a dermoid.

questions.question: Transformation of a mature cystic teratoma (dermoid) into malignancy is rare and typically results in:
questions.detail: The rare malignant transformation of a dermoid cyst typically produces Squamous Cell Carcinoma.

questions.question: What is the most common type of ovarian malignancy?
questions.detail: Serous tumors are the most frequent type of primary ovarian cancer.

questions.question: Serous ovarian tumors are characteristically described as:
choices: Large, unilocular (few septations), and frequently bilateral
questions.detail: Serous tumors are typically large, unilocular, and frequently affect both ovaries.

questions.question: 'Mucinous' ovarian tumors differ from serous ones because they are more likely to be:
choices: Multilocular (more septations) and associated with smoking
questions.detail: Mucinous tumors are typically multilocular and have a known association with smoking.

questions.question: A potential complication of a ruptured mucinous ovarian tumor is:
questions.detail: Rupture can lead to pseudomyxoma peritonei, with characteristic scalloping along liver and splenic margins.

questions.question: Which ovarian tumor is nicknamed 'Ovarian Transitional Cell Carcinoma'?
questions.detail: The Brenner tumor is sometimes referred to as 'Ovarian Transitional Cell Carcinoma'.

questions.question: Unlike ovarian fibromas, Brenner tumors characteristically demonstrate:
questions.detail: Unlike fibromas, calcifications are very common (seen in 80%) in Brenner tumors.

questions.question: 'Struma Ovarii' is a specialized subtype of ovarian teratoma that contains:
questions.detail: Struma ovarii is an ovarian teratoma that contains thyroid tissue.

questions.question: On MRI, the 'cystic' areas of a Struma Ovarii tumor will show:
choices: Very low T2 signal (from thick colloid)
questions.detail: A classic MRI finding is very low T2 signal in the 'cystic' areas, representing thick colloid.

questions.question: 'Meigs Syndrome' is defined by which clinical triad?
choices: Benign ovarian tumor (fibroma), ascites, and pleural effusion
questions.detail: Meigs syndrome is the specific association of an ovarian fibroma, ascites, and pleural effusion.

questions.question: On MRI, an ovarian fibroma classically appears:
choices: T1 and T2 dark with a 'black garland sign' (T2 dark rim)
questions.detail: Fibromas are T1/T2 dark and often demonstrate a 'black garland' of T2-darkness along the rim.

questions.question: A 'Krukenberg Tumor' refers to metastatic disease to the ovaries, typically from which primary site?
questions.detail: A Krukenberg tumor is a gastric or GI malignancy that has spread to the ovaries.

questions.question: What is the 'most constant finding' in the diagnosis of ovarian torsion?
questions.detail: While flow may vary, an enlarged ovary (> 4 cm) is the most constant imaging sign of torsion.

questions.question: Why does the presence of Doppler flow NOT exclude ovarian torsion?
choices: The ovary has a dual blood supply.
questions.detail: Because the ovary receives blood from both the uterine and ovarian arteries, flow may still be present despite torsion.

questions.question: The 'Cogwheel Appearance' on ultrasound refers to the longitudinal folds of a thickened:
questions.detail: The 'cogwheel appearance' is a buzzword for the thickened folds of a hydrosalpinx.

questions.question: The 'Waist Sign' is an imaging finding used to help differentiate:
questions.detail: The 'waist sign' is used to identify a tubular hydrosalpinx and distinguish it from a solid ovarian mass.

questions.question: On ultrasound, a 'Hydrosalpinx' appears as a:
choices: Thin (or thick) elongated tubular structure in the pelvis
questions.detail: Hydrosalpinx is characterized as a fluid-filled, elongated tubular structure in the adnexa.

questions.question: 'Pelvic Inflammatory Disease' (PID) is characteristically associated with which uterine finding on ultrasound?
questions.detail: The buzzword 'indefinite uterus' refers to the ill-defined uterine margins often seen in PID.

questions.question: 'Ovarian Vein Thrombophlebitis' (OVT) is most common postpartum and occurs on the RIGHT side in what percentage of cases?
questions.detail: OVT occurs on the right side in approximately 80% of postpartum cases.

questions.question: A 'Peritoneal Inclusion Cyst' is an inflammatory cyst that occurs when:
choices: A fetus develops in the abdomen.
questions.detail: These cysts form when pelvic adhesions trap fluid around a functioning ovary.

questions.question: A key feature of a peritoneal inclusion cyst is that its shape is:
questions.detail: Unlike simple cysts, peritoneal inclusion cysts are 'passive' and take the shape of surrounding pelvic organs.

questions.question: In Gestational Trophoblastic Disease, why is 'hyperemesis' (severe vomiting) often part of the history?
choices: Marked elevation of B-hCG causes vomiting.
It is caused by fetal movement.
It is due to low blood sugar.
questions.detail: Extremely high levels of b-HCG associated with molar pregnancies cause hyperemesis.

questions.question: A 'Complete Mole' characteristically has which karyotype?
questions.detail: A complete mole has a 46XX diploid karyotype.

questions.question: The classic first-trimester ultrasound appearance of a complete mole is described as:
questions.detail: The 'snowstorm' look describes an echogenic solid mass filling the uterus in early molar pregnancy.

questions.question: In the second trimester, a molar pregnancy takes on which classic appearance?
questions.detail: The 'bunch of grapes' appearance results from the expansion of individual vesicles within the mole.

questions.question: A 'Partial Mole' is characterized by being:
questions.detail: The partial mole characteristically has a triploid (69XXY) karyotype.

questions.question: 'Choriocarcinoma' is an aggressive malignancy that is suspected when serum b-HCG levels rise during which period?
choices: 8 to 10 weeks following evacuation of a molar pregnancy
questions.detail: Post-evacuation rise in b-HCG between weeks 8 and 10 is highly concerning for choriocarcinoma.

questions.question: Choriocarcinoma is unique because it spreads hematogenously (via blood) to almost anywhere, whereas most other testicular/genital tumors spread via:
questions.detail: Unlike most testicular or genital cancers that favor lymphatics, choriocarcinoma characteristically spreads via the bloodstream.

questions.question: In a cross-section of the penis, which structure contains the urethra?
questions.detail: The urethra is located within the corpus spongiosum.

questions.question: A 'Fractured Penis' is anatomically defined by a fracture/rupture of the:
choices: Corpus cavernosum and its surrounding tunica albuginea
questions.detail: Penile fracture involves the rupture of the corpus cavernosum and the tunica albuginea.

questions.question: Stigmata of a penile fracture injury can include 'Peyronie Disease,' which is:
choices: Suboptimal angulation from fibrous scar formation
questions.detail: Peyronie disease results from scarring, causing abnormal angulation of the penis.

questions.question: Which genetic syndrome features the association of 'Bilateral Solid Testicular Masses' and 'Adrenal Rests'?
questions.detail: Bilateral solid testicular masses in the setting of CAH are known as adrenal rests.

questions.question: 'Klinefelter Syndrome' (47XXY) is associated with a 300x increased risk for which tumor?
choices: Extra-gonadal Germ Cell Tumors (in the chest)
questions.detail: Klinefelter patients have a massively increased risk for germ cell tumors within the mediastinum.

questions.question: In an infant, a 'Sacrococcygeal Teratoma' is the most common tumor and is located:
choices: In the pelvis (often with a coccyx attachment)
questions.detail: Sacrococcygeal teratoma is the most common tumor of infancy and is found in the pelvic region.

questions.question: What is the 'Next Step' if you identify a unicornuate uterus on imaging?
choices: Ultrasound or CT the kidneys (to look for agenesis)
questions.detail: Unicornuate uterus has a 70% association with ipsilateral renal agenesis, so the kidneys must be evaluated.

questions.question: 'Mayer-Rokitansky-Kuster-Hauser' (MRKH) syndrome is characterized by Mullerian agenesis and:
questions.detail: A defining hallmark of MRKH is that the ovaries remain normal despite uterine and vaginal absence.

questions.question: 'DES exposure' in utero is the classic 'reflex generator' for which uterine anomaly?
questions.detail: In utero DES exposure is the classic cause for a T-shaped uterus.

questions.question: A 'Septate Uterus' is distinguished from a bicornuate one because the fundal contour is:
questions.detail: A septate uterus has a normal fundal contour, whereas a bicornuate one has a deep 'heart-shaped' cleft.

questions.question: Which uterine anomaly has the HIGHEST association with miscarriage?
questions.detail: The septate uterus carries the highest risk of miscarriage due to poor blood supply to the septum.

questions.question: On HSG, 'Salpingitis Isthmica Nodosa' (SIN) involves which part of the fallopian tube?
questions.detail: SIN typically involves the proximal 2/3 of the fallopian tubes.

questions.question: SIN on a board exam is the 'Aunt Minnie' for:
questions.detail: The finding of SIN is strongly associated with infertility and an increased risk of ectopic pregnancy.

questions.question: 'Uterine AVMs' are most commonly acquired after which event?
questions.detail: Most uterine AVMs result from prior intrauterine procedures like D&C or C-sections.

questions.question: Post-surgical 'Asherman Syndrome' is defined as clinical infertility resulting from:
questions.detail: Asherman Syndrome is infertility caused by scarring or adhesions within the uterine cavity.

questions.question: 'Endometritis' characteristically presents 2–5 days after delivery with:
choices: Fever, uterine tenderness, and thickened endometrium
questions.detail: Postpartum fever and tenderness are clinical markers for endometritis.

questions.question: In pelvic floor anatomy, the 'Anterior Compartment' contains the:
questions.detail: The bladder and urethra comprise the anterior pelvic compartment.

questions.question: A 'Cystocele' is defined as bladder descent greater than 1 cm below the:
questions.detail: Descent > 1 cm below the Pubococcygeal Line defines a cystocele.

questions.question: In dynamic pelvic MRI, the 'H Line' is used to measure:
questions.detail: The H line is a measurement of hiatal widening/enlargement.

questions.question: The 'M Line' on pelvic MRI measures:
questions.detail: The M line represents the vertical descent of the pelvic floor from the PCL.

questions.question: 'Uterine Prolapse' is defined as descent of the cervix greater than 1 cm below the:
questions.detail: Uterine prolapse is when the cervix drops > 1 cm below the PCL.

questions.question: 'Red (Carneous) Degeneration' of a fibroid characteristically occurs during pregnancy and shows which MRI sign?
choices: A peripheral rim of high T1 signal
questions.detail: The peripheral T1-bright rim (blood/methemoglobin) is the hallmark of red degeneration.

questions.question: On T2-weighted MRI, the 'Junctional Zone' of the uterus is the:
questions.detail: The junctional zone is the distinct dark inner myometrial layer on T2 images.

questions.question: 'Adenomyosis' is diagnosed when the T2-dark junctional zone measures greater than:
questions.detail: A thickness > 12 mm for the junctional zone is diagnostic of adenomyosis.

questions.question: Endometrial thickness in a postmenopausal woman with BLEEDING should be biopsied if it exceeds:
questions.detail: In a postmenopausal bleeder, endometrium > 4–5 mm requires biopsy to exclude cancer.

questions.question: 'Lynch Syndrome' (HNPCC) carries a 30–50x increased risk for which cancer?
questions.detail: Lynch syndrome is associated with a massive increased risk for endometrial cancer.

questions.question: 'Tamoxifen' use for breast cancer is associated with which endometrial changes?
questions.detail: Tamoxifen use characteristically leads to subendometrial cysts and endometrial polyps.

questions.question: In MRI staging of endometrial cancer, Stage 1b is defined as invasion of:
questions.detail: Stage 1b represents deep tumor invasion (more than 50%) into the myometrium.

questions.question: In cervical cancer, 'Stage 2b' is significant because it involves:
questions.detail: Stage 2b (parametrial invasion) changes management to chemo-radiation rather than surgery.

questions.question: Parametrial invasion is identified on MRI by the disruption of the:
choices: T2 dark ring of the cervix
questions.detail: Invasion is called when the T2-dark cervical stroma (the dark ring) is disrupted.

questions.question: 'Vaginal Rhabdomyosarcoma' is the most common vaginal tumor in children and typically presents as:
questions.detail: In children, this tumor appears as a solid T2-bright enhancing mass in the lower uterus or vagina.

questions.question: A metastasis to the ANTERIOR wall upper 1/3 of the vagina is 90% likely from the:
questions.detail: Mets to the anterior upper vagina almost always come from the genital tract (cervix/uterus).

questions.question: A metastasis to the POSTERIOR wall lower 1/3 of the vagina is 90% likely from the:
questions.detail: Mets to the posterior lower vagina almost always originate from the GI tract.

questions.question: 'Nabothian Cysts' are mucous retention cysts located on the:
questions.detail: Nabothian cysts are common benign cysts located on the cervix.

questions.question: 'Gartner Duct Cysts' are remnants of the Wolffian ducts and are found in the:
choices: Anterior lateral wall of the upper vagina
Posterior wall of the lower vagina
questions.detail: Gartner duct cysts are classically located along the anterolateral wall of the proximal (upper) vagina.

questions.question: Which vaginal cyst is found below the pubic symphysis, 'near the butthole'?
questions.detail: Bartholin cysts are located distal to the symphysis, in the vulvar region.

questions.question: 'Skene Gland Cysts' are located in the periurethral area and can cause:
questions.detail: Because they are periurethral, Skene gland cysts can cause obstructive urinary symptoms and UTIs.

questions.question: 'Intravasation' during an HSG refers to:
choices: Contrast backflow into the venous or lymphatic system
questions.detail: Intravasation is the backflow of contrast from the uterine cavity into the pelvic veins or lymphatics.

questions.question: 'Pelvic Congestion Syndrome' is associated with dilated gonadal veins measuring greater than:
questions.detail: A gonadal vein diameter > 10 mm is diagnostic for pelvic congestion.

questions.question: Symptoms of Pelvic Congestion Syndrome classically IMPROVE after:
questions.detail: Once estrogen levels drop at menopause, pelvic congestion symptoms typically resolve.

questions.question: Uterine Artery Embolization (UAE) is most successful for which fibroid type?
questions.detail: Cellular fibroids (demonstrated by high T2 signal) respond best to embolization.

questions.question: A known 5% risk following a UAE procedure is:
choices: DVT / PE (from pelvic vein compression release)
questions.detail: Shrinking the large uterus can release compressed veins and trigger a DVT or PE.

questions.question: In a patient with Turner Syndrome (XO), the ovaries are typically:
questions.detail: Turner syndrome features small, 'streaky' ovaries and a prepubertal uterus.

questions.question: The 'most common' cause of intrauterine adhesions (Asherman's) is:
questions.detail: Intrauterine scarring is most frequently the result of trauma from a prior D&C.

questions.question: If you see a unicornuate uterus with a non-communicating rudimentary horn, the major risk is:
questions.detail: Pregnancy in a non-communicating horn nearly always results in a life-threatening rupture.

questions.question: 'Transformation' logic for board exams: An endometrioma turns into Clear Cell, and a Dermoid turns into:
questions.detail: Endometriomas rarely transform into clear cell; dermoids rarely transform into squamous cell CA.
